question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential execution of NetworkList() followed by NetworkStatus() in checkNetworkStatus(), where a network partition occurring between the two calls causes NetworkStatus() to fail while NetworkList() succeeded, resulting in an inconsistent health state that confuses orchestrators?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() calls NetworkStatus() using only networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] without validating the identifier, by causing the /network/list endpoint to return a manipulated identifier that makes NetworkStatus() query a different network, masking a real partition on the intended network?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on the 'network' health.Config to perform a time-based denial-of-service, where each GET /health/readiness request holds a goroutine for up to 10 seconds waiting for the loopback call, and enough concurrent requests exhaust the goroutine pool?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on the 'postgresql' health.Config combined with the 10-second Timeout on the 'network' health.Config to hold each readiness check goroutine for up to 20 seconds, amplifying the DoS impact of concurrent health probe flooding?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rosettaClient is created once at NewHealthController() time with a fixed serverUrl of http://localhost:{port}, meaning if the port changes after initialization (e.g., due to a config reload), checkNetworkStatus() will always fail and permanently report a network partition?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the static rosettaClient created in checkNetworkStatus() — which is shared across all concurrent health check invocations — to cause race conditions in the HTTP client's connection pool under high concurrency, leading to spurious network partition reports?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of authentication on the loopback /network/list call in checkNetworkStatus() to perform a response spoofing attack in a multi-tenant environment, returning a crafted NetworkList response that causes networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] to panic or return wrong data?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GET /health/readiness endpoint to perform a side-channel timing attack, measuring the response latency to determine whether the network partition check timed out (close to 10 seconds) or failed fast, inferring the nature of the partition without any authentication?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate the HTTP status code of the /network/list response before parsing it, meaning a server returning HTTP 500 with a valid JSON body could be parsed as a successful response, masking a real network partition?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a circuit breaker in checkNetworkStatus() to cause the health check to repeatedly attempt loopback connections during a sustained network partition, generating excessive log volume via log.Errorf() that fills disk and causes secondary failures?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the context passed to the inner func(ctx context.Context) in checkNetworkStatus() is the health framework's context, which may be cancelled if the outer GET /health/readiness request is aborted by the client, causing the NetworkList() call to be cancelled and falsely reported as a network partition error?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit HTTP/1.1 keep-alive behavior on the loopback connection in checkNetworkStatus() to hold a persistent connection to the server, preventing new connections from being established during a network partition recovery, delaying the health check from returning to healthy status?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() makes two sequential HTTP calls (NetworkList then NetworkStatus) with no atomicity guarantee, so that a network partition occurring between the two calls causes an inconsistent health report where the node appears partially available, confusing load balancers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that both readiness checks ('postgresql' and 'network') run with SkipOnErr: false, meaning an attacker who can induce either a DB connection failure or a loopback network failure can force the entire readiness check to fail, simulating a network partition even if only one component is affected?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated GET /health/readiness endpoint to perform reconnaissance during a network partition, determining from the JSON response which specific check ('postgresql' or 'network') is failing, and using that information to identify the partition boundary?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fmt.Sprintf('http://localhost:%d', port) uses the uint16 port value directly, and if port is 0 (zero-value Config), the URL becomes http://localhost:0, causing checkNetworkStatus() to always fail and permanently report a network partition, which could be triggered by a config injection attack?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient in checkNetworkStatus() uses the 'readiness-check' user-agent string with no authentication, allowing any process listening on localhost:{port} (e.g., after a port hijack in a shared environment) to intercept the health check and return fake responses that mask a real network partition?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate validation on the loopback HTTP call in checkNetworkStatus() to perform a protocol downgrade attack in environments where TLS is expected, causing the health check to succeed over an insecure channel while the real TLS-protected network is partitioned?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not distinguish between a connection refused error (server down) and a timeout error (network partition), causing both conditions to be reported identically as network partition failures, preventing operators from diagnosing the true cause?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared rosettaClient HTTP transport in checkNetworkStatus() to cause connection pool exhaustion by sending many concurrent GET /health/readiness requests, each of which opens a new loopback connection, until the OS TCP connection limit is reached and the node falsely reports a network partition?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() logs errors via log.Errorf() on every failed probe during a network partition, causing log flooding that consumes CPU and I/O resources, degrading server performance and making the partition recovery slower?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GET /health/liveness endpoint — which always returns HTTP 200 with no checks — to confirm the process is alive during a network partition, using this as a signal to time attacks that exploit the partitioned state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check context in checkNetworkStatus() has no deadline beyond the 10-second Timeout set by health.Config, meaning if the health framework does not enforce the timeout correctly, the loopback HTTP call could hang indefinitely during a network partition, blocking the health check goroutine forever?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() calls rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkList() which internally may follow HTTP redirects, and by injecting a redirect response on the loopback interface, cause the health check to connect to an external host, leaking the 'readiness-check' user-agent and internal port information?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] is accessed without bounds checking, and during a network partition the /network/list endpoint may return a partial or empty response, causing a runtime panic that crashes the health check and prevents recovery detection?
